title: Fixed-Boost Wigner Noise: Strict Trace-Distance Contraction without Quantum Degradability
abstract: A Lorentz boost acts on the canonical spin of a massive particle through a momentum-dependent Wigner rotation. We show that, for one fixed observer boost, reducing over an uncertain momentum can strictly contract every pairwise spin-state trace distance without producing a channel that is degradable from the less contracted one. For spin $1/2$, we first characterize the exact inversion-symmetric channel cone generated by a fixed Wigner angle and transverse momentum directions. Inside this cone lies the Pauli family $M_α=\operatorname{diag}(1-α,1-α,1-2α)$, $0\leqα<1/2$. For $0<α<β<1/2$, all trace distances between distinct spin states are strictly smaller after $M_β$ than after $M_α$, yet the unique linear post-processing factor has a negative normalized Choi eigenvalue. We solve the optimization over all physical converters exactly: $\frac{1}{2}\inf_{Λ\in\mathrm{CPTP}}\|Φ_β-Λ\circΦ_α\|_\diamond=\frac{α(β-α)}{2-3α}$, whereas the reverse deficiency is $β-α$. Thus the identity dominates the family, while all positive-noise members are pairwise incomparable under CPTP post-processing. The ideal construction is realized as the narrow-packet limit of pure, normalizable five-component momentum states, and explicit perturbation and finite-shot tomography bounds certify an open set of examples. Separately, every nonidentity member fails embedding in a time-homogeneous Pauli-diagonal Lindblad semigroup. Hence ordering all unassisted spin distinguishabilities does not determine the quantum statistical post-processing order.
comment: 10 pages, 3 figures. Ancillary verification code included

bite.hook: Physicists prove that ordering spin distinguishability does not determine quantum statistical post-processing order.
tldr: They analyzed how a fixed Lorentz boost and uncertain momentum affect the spin state of a massive particle via Wigner rotation.
They characterized the exact inversion-symmetric channel cone for spin 1/2 and solved the optimization over all physical converters.
They found that trace distances strictly contract without creating a degradable channel, meaning noise members are pairwise incomparable.
bite.whyItMatters: This work reveals a fundamental gap in quantum information theory, proving that just because one quantum channel preserves state distinguishability better than another, it does not mean we can physically transform the better channel into the worse one.
bite.eli5: Imagine you have two blurry cameras. Camera A is slightly clearer than Camera B for every picture you take, so you might think you could easily add a little extra blur to Camera A's photos to perfectly mimic Camera B. This study proves that in the quantum world, you sometimes cannot do this—even though one camera is strictly better, there is no physical way to transition from the better one to the worse one.

keyNumbers.label: Spin value
keyNumbers.context: The spin-1/2 system analyzed to characterize the exact inversion-symmetric channel cone.

glossary.shortDef: A relativistic effect where a Lorentz boost changes the spin direction of a massive particle.
glossary.eli5Def: When something moves close to the speed of light, its quantum spin direction gets tilted in a weird way because of relativity.

glossary.shortDef: A metric measuring the distinguishability between two quantum states.
glossary.eli5Def: A mathematical ruler that tells you how easy or hard it is to tell two quantum states apart.

glossary.shortDef: The property where a quantum channel can be post-processed to simulate its environment's output.
glossary.eli5Def: A property where you can perfectly recreate the lost, noisy information of a system using only the output you received.

glossary.shortDef: An eigenvalue of the Choi matrix representing a quantum channel, used to check physical realizability.
glossary.eli5Def: A special number that helps physicists check if a mathematical operation on quantum states is actually possible in the real world.
